summary: DeVault explains why he uses an 11-year-old Thinkpad X200 as his main laptop and keeps older hardware generally. He argues that using old hardware forces developers to empathize with users who can't afford high-end machines, and demonstrates that modern software often bloats to fill available resources unnecessarily. He contrasts his lightweight sway desktop booting in under 30 seconds with KDE taking 10x longer on the same hardware. His core position is that a machine capable of billions of operations per second for $60 should be sufficient for nearly any task.
key_points: The Thinkpad X200 has full upstream Linux kernel support, works with niche OSes and coreboot, and is durable and user-serviceable after 11 years
Software tends to grow to consume whatever hardware you give it, and developers using enthusiast-grade hardware lose sight of real-world user constraints
Most computing tasks outside specialized domains like video encoding and machine learning don't actually require high-end hardware
Using old hardware is a deliberate practice in empathy — it forces developers to confront the experience of users with limited resources
A $60 computer capable of compiling the Linux kernel in 20 minutes and playing 1080p video is sufficient for nearly any problem
money_quotes: It forces me to empathise with users who can't use high-end hardware.
Software often grows to use the hardware you give it.
Software developers tend to be computer enthusiasts, and use enthusiast-grade hardware.
This high-end hardware isn't really necessary for most applications outside of video encoding, machine learning, and a few other domains.
Many users cannot afford high-end computer hardware, and most have better things to spend their money on.
If I can get a computer which can do nearly 5 billion operations per second for $60, that should be sufficient to solve nearly any problem.
key_insight: Developers should deliberately use modest hardware to maintain empathy with ordinary users and resist the tendency for software to bloat into available resources.
